content.full_text:
February 22, 1991


Tracey S. Buck-Walsh
DIEPENBROCK, WULFF, PLANT AND HANNEGAN
300 Capitol Mall, Suite 1700
Post Office Box 3034
Sacramento, CA 95812-3034

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-91-075

Dear Ms. Buck-Walsh:

     This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Attorney General Dan Lungren with respect to his responsibilities
under the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/

QUESTION

     What is the proper method for Attorney General Lungren to
establish a 1994 campaign committee under the Political Reform
Act?

CONCLUSION

     Consistent with the modified advice we are now providing, the
Attorney General should transfer his funds into his 1994 account,
terminate the 1990 account, and amend his statement of
organization to reflect that the committee and existing
identification number are for his 1994 reelection, or delete
reference to a year of election from the statement of organization
entirely.




1/  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
section 18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-075
Page 2


FACTS

Attorney General Lungren was elected to the office of State
Attorney General in 1990. His controlled committee, Lungren for
Attorney General 1990, continues to collect funds for the 1990
election. The Attorney General now wishes to set up his 1994
campaign committee. You stated that based on advice obtained from
his office, the Attorney General filed a candidate intention
statement (Form 501) and campaign bank account statement (Form
502) and established a separate campaign bank account for the 1994
election, but was under the impression that the 1990 committee and
identification number could be used for the new bank account.
Consequently, the Attorney General currently has two campaign bank
accounts and a single campaign committee.

ANALYSIS

On September 25, 1990, the United States District Court
issued an order in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO,
et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (1990) 747 F.Supp.
580 ("SEIU"), which invalidated portions of the Act added by
Proposition 73 in June of 1988. These provisions included the
fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act and the transfer
ban of Section 85304.2/

However, the campaign bank account sections of Proposition 73
were not affected by the court's decision. (Sections 85200 -
85202.) Section 85201 provides that all contributions or loans
made to a candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee
shall be deposited in a single campaign bank account. The
Commission has interpreted this to mean that a candidate for
elective office may have only one campaign bank account and one
controlled committee for each campaign.3/ (Regulation 18521;
Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-409, copies enclosed.)

In light of the changes caused by the federal court order, on
January 14, 1991, the Commission began advising that a candidate
may redesignate a campaign committee and campaign bank account
established after January 1, 1989, for reelection to the same
office at a future date. Thus, the 1990 campaign bank account and

___________________________
2/ Please note, the court order did not invalidate the special
election and special election contribution limits and
transfer ban of Proposition 73. Therefore, these restrictions
remain operative in both local and statewide special elections.
Moreover, please be aware that the decision in SEIU is being
appealed.

3/ The Commission has created a narrow exception with respect to
candidate controlled ballot measure committees which is not an
issue in this letter. (Olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363, copy
enclosed.)

Our File No. A-91-075
Page 3


campaign committee of Attorney General Lungren may both be
redesignated for the 1994 election. This would be accomplished by
filing a candidate intention statement (Form 501) for the future
election, and by amending the campaign bank account statement
(Form 502) and statement of organization (Form 410). This is true
even if the committee and account have campaign funds and/or
outstanding debts from the previous election.

According to his facts, the Attorney General has two campaign
bank accounts and only one campaign committee and one
identification number. Consistent with the modified advice we are
now providing, the Attorney General should transfer his funds into
his 1994 account, terminate the 1990 account and amend his
statement of organization to reflect that the committee and
existing identification number are for the 1994 election, or
delete reference to a year of election from the statement of
organization entirely.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
    Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JW:aa

Enclosures

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

Consequently, the Attorney General currently has two campaign bank accounts and a single campaign committee.
sections.analysis:
On September 25, 1990, the United States District Court issued an order in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (1990) 747 F.Supp. 580 ("SEIU"), which invalidated portions of the Act added by Proposition 73 in June of 1988. These provisions included the fiscal year contribution limitations of the Act and the transfer ban of Section 85304.2/

However, the campaign bank account sections of Proposition 73 were not affected by the court's decision. (Sections 85200 - 85202.) Section 85201 provides that all contributions or loans made to a candidate, or to the candidate's controlled committee shall be deposited in a single campaign bank account. The Commission has interpreted this to mean that a candidate for elective office may have only one campaign bank account and one controlled committee for each campaign.3/ (Regulation 18521; Riddle Advice Letter, No. A-88-409, copies enclosed.)

In light of the changes caused by the federal court order, on January 14, 1991, the Commission began advising that a candidate may redesignate a campaign committee and campaign bank account established after January 1, 1989, for reelection to the same office at a future date. Thus, the 1990 campaign bank account and

2/ Please note, the court order did not invalidate the special election and special runoff election contribution limits and transfer ban of Proposition 73. Therefore, these restrictions remain operative in both local and statewide special elections. Moreover, please be aware that the decision in SEIU is being appealed.

3/ The Commission has created a narrow exception with respect to candidate controlled ballot measure committees which is not an issue in this letter. (Olson Advice Letter, No. A-89-363, copy enclosed.)

campaign committee of Attorney General Lungren may both be redesignated for the 1994 election. This would be accomplished by filing a candidate intention statement (Form 501) for the future election, and by amending the campaign bank account statement (Form 502) and statement of organization (Form 410). This is true even if the committee and account have campaign funds and/or outstanding debts from the previous election.

According to his facts, the Attorney General has two campaign bank accounts and only one campaign committee and one identification number. Consistent with the modified advice we are now providing, the Attorney General should transfer his funds into his 1994 account, terminate the 1990 account and amend his statement of organization to reflect that the committee and existing identification number are for the 1994 election, or delete reference to a year of election from the statement of organization entirely.
